enrichment.short_description: Piwigo is a free, open-source, self-hosted photo gallery and image management platform for organizing, tagging and publishing photo collections on the web, with an optional paid cloud-hosting service at Piwigo.com.
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (small core team, historically around 10 members)
enrichment.seo_title: Piwigo Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Piwigo review: open-source, self-hosted photo gallery software with albums, plugins and cloud hosting. See features, pricing and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Piwigo is a free, open-source photo management and gallery platform originally released in 2002 by French developer Pierrick Le Gall (as an evolution of his earlier PhpWebGallery project started in 2001). It is distributed under the GNU GPL and can be self-hosted on any PHP and MySQL/MariaDB web server, or run as a managed cloud service through Piwigo.com, a separate hosting business operated by the same team (PigoLabs SAS, based near Nantes, France) that funds ongoing development of the open-source core. Piwigo lets individuals and organizations organize photos into nested albums and categories, apply granular per-album and per-group permissions, batch-manage large libraries, and extend functionality through more than 200 community plugins and customizable themes. It supports uploads from desktop tools like digiKam, Shotwell and Lightroom, plus official mobile apps, and its interface has been translated into a wide range of languages by its community. Piwigo differentiates itself from consumer photo apps like Google Photos or Flickr by targeting users who want full data ownership and self-hosting control, and from other open-source galleries (such as Coppermine or Zenphoto) through its long development history, active plugin ecosystem and dedicated commercial hosting option. The self-hosted software is entirely free; Piwigo.com's managed hosting is billed annually across four tiers ranging from a Pro plan at EUR29/month (10 GB storage, one administrator) up to a VIP plan at EUR399/month (1000 GB storage, unlimited administrators and phone support), with additional storage add-ons available on every tier. Piwigo is used by museums, universities, tourism boards, research institutions, photography studios and community organizations that need a durable, extensible photo archive rather than a disposable social feed. After more than two decades of continuous development, over 130 releases and roughly 2.8 million downloads, Piwigo remains a niche but stable choice for teams that value openness and control. Its main alternatives include SmugMug, Zenfolio, Pixieset and Flickr for hosted options, and Coppermine or Zenphoto among other self-hosted, open-source galleries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Piwigo is open-source photo management software that lets individuals and organizations organize, tag and publish photo collections on the web. First released in 2002 by French developer Pierrick Le Gall, it grew out of an earlier project called PhpWebGallery and was renamed Piwigo in 2009.

The software can be downloaded and self-hosted for free on any PHP-compatible server, or run through Piwigo.com, a separate paid cloud-hosting service operated by the same team for people who prefer not to manage their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Piwigo organizes photos into nested albums and categories with granular, group-based permissions, making it suited to teams that need to control exactly who can view, upload to or administer specific collections.

Its functionality can be extended through more than 200 community-built plugins and themes, and photos can be uploaded from desktop tools such as digiKam, Shotwell and Lightroom, or from Piwigo's own mobile apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Piwigo software is free and open source under the GNU GPL, with no cost for self-hosting on your own server.
Piwigo.com's managed cloud hosting is billed annually across four tiers, from Pro at EUR29/month up to VIP at EUR399/month, with additional storage available as an add-on on every plan.

features.title: Album and category hierarchy
features.description: Organize photos into nested albums, categories and tags for large libraries.

features.title: Batch photo management
features.description: Upload, edit metadata and manage permissions for many photos at once.

features.title: Plugin and theme ecosystem
features.description: Extend functionality with 200+ community plugins and customizable themes.

features.title: Granular permissions
features.description: Control who can view, upload to or administer specific albums and groups.

features.title: Desktop and mobile uploaders
features.description: Upload directly from digiKam, Shotwell, Lightroom or the official Piwigo mobile apps.

features.title: Piwigo.com managed hosting
features.description: Optional paid cloud hosting removes the need to self-host and maintain servers.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: The software and community translations support a wide range of languages.

pros: Free and open source with full source code access and no vendor lock-in
Highly extensible through plugins and themes
Granular album and group permission controls suited to teams and organizations
Mature project with over two decades of continuous development
Optional managed hosting removes server maintenance burden
cons: Self-hosted setup requires PHP/MySQL server knowledge and ongoing maintenance
Interface feels dated compared to newer, design-forward gallery tools
Piwigo.com hosting is billed annually only, with no monthly option currently
Smaller ecosystem and community than mainstream competitors like SmugMug or Flickr

faqs.question: Is Piwigo free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Piwigo software is free and open source under the GNU GPL; you can self-host it on your own server at no cost. Piwigo.com offers optional paid managed hosting starting at EUR29/month.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Piwigo.org and Piwigo.com?
faqs.answer: Piwigo.org distributes the free, self-hosted open-source software; Piwigo.com is a separate paid cloud-hosting service run by the same team for people who do not want to manage their own server.

faqs.question: What server requirements does self-hosted Piwigo have?
faqs.answer: Piwigo requires a PHP-compatible web host with a MySQL or MariaDB database.

faqs.question: Does Piwigo support video and audio files?
faqs.answer: Yes, video and audio are supported on Piwigo.com plans, and all file formats are supported from the Team tier upward.

faqs.question: Can I migrate away from Piwigo if I stop using it?
faqs.answer: Yes, because Piwigo is open source and self-hostable, you retain full access to your database and files.

faqs.question: Does Piwigo have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, official apps and third-party uploaders including pLoader, digiKam, Shotwell and Lightroom plugins can upload photos to a Piwigo gallery.

faqs.question: Who maintains Piwigo?
faqs.answer: Piwigo was created by French developer Pierrick Le Gall and is maintained by a small core team plus community contributors, funded partly by the Piwigo.com hosting business.

faqs.question: What are Piwigo's main alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Coppermine, Zenphoto, SmugMug, Flickr and Pixieset, depending on whether self-hosting or a hosted service is preferred.

use_cases.description: Clubs, schools and tourism boards use Piwigo to let members or contributors upload and browse shared event photos.

features: Unlimited photos, limited only by your own server

enrichment.short_description: Pixelfed is a free, open-source, federated photo-sharing platform built on ActivityPub, offering an ad-free, chronological, Instagram-like experience across independently run Fediverse servers.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted or join a public instance); third-party managed hosting from about $19/month
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (small independent open-source project)
enrichment.headquarters: Not publicly disclosed (founder based in Canada)
enrichment.seo_title: Pixelfed Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pixelfed review: the open-source, federated Instagram alternative on ActivityPub. Explore features, hosting costs and top Fediverse alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pixelfed is a free, open-source, federated photo and video-sharing platform released in December 2018 by Canadian developer Daniel Supernault. It is licensed under AGPLv3+ and built on ActivityPub, the same open protocol used by Mastodon, PeerTube and Friendica, which lets Pixelfed accounts interoperate with those platforms as part of the broader Fediverse. Rather than being one centrally owned service, Pixelfed is a piece of software that anyone can self-host, and users typically join a public instance such as pixelfed.social, or a smaller community-run server whose administrator sets its own moderation policy. The platform is explicitly positioned as an ethical, privacy-respecting alternative to Instagram: it shows no advertising, does not sell user data, avoids third-party analytics, and defaults to reverse-chronological feeds instead of an engagement-ranking algorithm. Features include posts of up to ten photos or videos, Stories, curated collections, hashtags, geo-tagging, two-factor authentication, and post-level audience controls (public, followers-only or unlisted). Official native iOS and Android apps launched in January 2025, expanding access beyond the web client. Because Pixelfed has no venture-capital backing or advertising revenue, it is funded through donations, Patreon sponsorships from users, and grants such as NGI Zero; individual instance operators, including the flagship pixelfed.social, cover their own hosting costs and have periodically reported funding shortfalls relative to expenses. Supernault also created Loops, a federated, TikTok-like short-video platform built on similar principles, extending the same decentralized, non-commercial model to video. Pixelfed targets privacy-conscious individuals, niche and regional communities, and open-web advocates who want an Instagram-style experience without corporate ownership, algorithmic feeds or advertising, as well as organizations that want to self-host their own photo-sharing community. Its closest comparison point is Instagram itself, though Pixelfed is better understood as competing with other Fediverse and independent photo platforms; relevant alternatives and adjacent products include Mastodon (for the broader federated social experience), PeerTube, Flickr and newer independent photography-social apps such as Glass.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pixelfed is free, open-source software for federated photo and video sharing, created by Canadian developer Daniel Supernault and first released in December 2018 under the AGPLv3+ license.

Instead of one central company running the service, Pixelfed is a protocol-based network: anyone can self-host an instance, and users typically sign up on a public server such as pixelfed.social or a smaller community-run instance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Pixelfed uses ActivityPub, the same open protocol behind Mastodon, PeerTube and Friendica, so Pixelfed accounts can follow and interact with people on those platforms as part of the Fediverse.
It offers Instagram-style features such as multi-photo posts, Stories and collections, but defaults to a chronological feed with no engagement-ranking algorithm, no ads and no third-party trackers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pixelfed itself is free: the software is open source, and joining a public instance costs nothing, with servers typically funded by donations from their communities.
For those who want a dedicated or private instance without self-managing a server, third-party managed hosting providers offer Pixelfed instances starting at roughly $19/month.

features.title: Federated ActivityPub network
features.description: Pixelfed accounts can interact with Mastodon, Friendica, PeerTube and other Fediverse platforms.

features.title: Chronological, algorithm-free feed
features.description: Posts appear in reverse-chronological order rather than being ranked by an engagement algorithm.

features.title: No ads or third-party trackers
features.description: The platform does not sell user data or show advertising.

features.title: Stories and collections
features.description: Supports ephemeral stories and curated photo or video collections similar to mainstream apps.

features.title: Self-hostable instances
features.description: Anyone can run their own Pixelfed server and moderate their own community.

features.title: Native iOS and Android apps
features.description: Official mobile apps launched in January 2025 alongside the web app.

features.title: Privacy controls
features.description: Post-level audience settings, two-factor authentication and no invasive analytics.

pros: Free, open source and AGPL-licensed with no corporate ownership
No ads, algorithmic manipulation or data resale
Interoperates with the broader Fediverse, including Mastodon and PeerTube
Users can choose or self-host an instance aligned with their values
Chronological feed favored by users wanting less algorithmic noise
cons: Smaller user base and network effects than Instagram
Instance funding relies heavily on donations, creating sustainability risk for some servers
Feature parity with Instagram, such as advanced discovery and shopping, is still developing
Choosing and trusting the right instance administrator adds friction for new users
Self-hosting requires server administration skills

faqs.question: Is Pixelfed free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pixelfed is free, open-source software; joining a public instance like pixelfed.social is free, and instance operators fund servers through donations.

faqs.question: Who created Pixelfed?
faqs.answer: Canadian developer Daniel Supernault created Pixelfed and released it in December 2018.

faqs.question: What is the Fediverse?
faqs.answer: The Fediverse is a network of independently operated, interoperable social platforms, including Pixelfed, Mastodon, PeerTube and Friendica, that communicate using the open ActivityPub protocol.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Pixelfed?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pixelfed is AGPLv3+ licensed and can be self-hosted on your own server, or you can join an existing public or community instance.

faqs.question: Does Pixelfed have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, official iOS and Android apps launched in January 2025.

faqs.question: How is Pixelfed funded?
faqs.answer: Through donations, Patreon sponsorships and grants such as NGI Zero, rather than venture capital or advertising.

faqs.question: What is Loops and how does it relate to Pixelfed?
faqs.answer: Loops is a federated, TikTok-like short video platform also created by Daniel Supernault, built on similar Fediverse principles.

faqs.question: How is Pixelfed different from Instagram?
faqs.answer: Pixelfed is open source, decentralized, ad-free and chronological, while Instagram is a centralized, ad-supported, algorithmically ranked platform owned by Meta.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious photo sharing

use_cases.title: Federated brand or creator presence
use_cases.description: Creators and organizations that want to participate in the open Fediverse alongside Mastodon use Pixelfed for visual content.

features: Join any public instance for free
Managed server setup for a small instance
Maintenance and updates handled by the provider
Suited to running a private or small community instance

enrichment.short_description: Pixpa is an all-in-one website builder for photographers, artists and creatives, combining portfolio sites, client proofing galleries, blogging and a zero-commission online store in one subscription.
enrichment.headquarters: New Delhi, India (with a US entity registered in Newark, Delaware)
enrichment.seo_title: Pixpa Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pixpa review: portfolio website builder for photographers and artists with client galleries, e-commerce and blogging. Compare pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pixpa is an integrated website-building platform designed specifically for photographers, artists and other visual creatives. The company was incorporated in the United States in January 2011 as Pixpa Inc., with an Indian entity following a year later, and is today headquartered in New Delhi, India, operating as a bootstrapped, profitable business rather than one dependent on venture funding; public reporting has put its team at roughly 20 people generating around $2.8 million in annual revenue. Pixpa's core proposition is consolidation: instead of paying separately for a portfolio site, a client-proofing tool, a print or product store and a blog, creatives get all four in a single subscription. The platform offers over 200 designer-made, responsive templates with a visual, no-code editor; client galleries that support proofing, commenting and multi-user favoriting for photo delivery and approval; a zero-commission online store for selling prints, digital downloads, products and services (with automated print fulfillment through WHCC lab integration); and an integrated blog with built-in SEO tools. Its customer base spans wedding, portrait and commercial photographers, illustrators, fine artists, graphic and UI/UX designers, architects, fashion designers, models, musicians and writers who need a professional online presence and, often, e-commerce capability. Pixpa differentiates itself from generic website builders through this creative-industry focus, a no-commission store policy, and an emphasis on human customer support, offering 24/7 live chat staffed by people rather than bots; it holds a 4.7-out-of-5 rating on Capterra across 600+ reviews. Pricing runs on four annual subscription tiers, from Basic at $5.40/month up to Advanced at $15/month, plus a standalone Client Gallery plan from $5/month for photographers who only need proofing; all plans include a 15-day free trial with no credit card required and a 30-day money-back guarantee, and annual subscribers get a free domain for the first year. Students and educators can receive discounts of up to 55 percent. Pixpa's main alternatives include Squarespace, Format, SmugMug, Zenfolio and Pixieset, with SmugMug, Zenfolio and Pixieset being the closest photography-specific competitors and Squarespace and Format representing broader design-led website builders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pixpa is an all-in-one website builder built specifically for photographers, artists and creative professionals, combining a portfolio site, client proofing galleries, an online store and a blog in one subscription.

Founded in 2011 and headquartered in New Delhi, India, Pixpa has grown as a bootstrapped company focused on serving visual creatives who need both a professional presentation and the ability to sell their work online.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform offers over 200 responsive, designer-made templates with a visual, drag-and-drop editor that requires no coding, alongside client galleries built for proofing, commenting and image delivery.

Its built-in store lets creatives sell prints, digital downloads, products and services without Pixpa taking a commission, and an integrated blog with SEO tools supports organic discovery for a creative's portfolio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pixpa offers four annual subscription tiers, from Basic at $5.40/month up to Advanced at $15/month, plus a standalone Client Gallery plan starting at $5/month for photographers who only need proofing.
All plans include a 15-day free trial with no credit card required and a 30-day money-back guarantee, and annual subscribers receive a free custom domain for the first year.

faqs.question: What is Pixpa used for?
faqs.answer: Pixpa is used to build portfolio websites, client proofing galleries, blogs and online stores, primarily for photographers, artists and other visual creatives.

faqs.question: Does Pixpa charge commission on store sales?
faqs.answer: No, Pixpa does not take a commission on sales made through its built-in e-commerce store.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pixpa offers a 15-day free trial with no credit card required, plus a 30-day money-back guarantee.

faqs.question: Can I use Pixpa just for client galleries?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pixpa offers a standalone Client Gallery plan starting at $5/month for photographers who only need proofing galleries.

faqs.question: Do students get a discount?
faqs.answer: Yes, students and educators can receive discounts of up to 55 percent.

faqs.question: Where is Pixpa based?
faqs.answer: Pixpa is headquartered in New Delhi, India, with a US corporate entity registered in Delaware.

faqs.question: What are Pixpa's main alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Squarespace, Format, SmugMug, Zenfolio and Pixieset.

faqs.question: Does Pixpa include a free domain?
faqs.answer: Yes, annual subscribers receive a free custom domain for the first year.

enrichment.short_description: Places Scout is a local SEO software platform that gives agencies and multi-location brands automated rank tracking, Google Business Profile monitoring, review and reputation tracking, and citation analysis across 400+ data points per business.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom (contact sales for a package quote)
enrichment.seo_title: Places Scout Review 2026: Features, Pricing, Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Places Scout review: local SEO software for rank tracking, Google Business Profile monitoring, reviews and citations. See features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Places Scout is a local SEO software platform that provides automated local search intelligence for businesses managing one or many physical locations. The company describes itself as tracking over 400 data points per business and has been in operation since 2011, based on customer testimonials referencing that founding date, though it does not publicly disclose headquarters, team size or funding information. Places Scout is built primarily for digital marketing agencies managing portfolios of local-business clients and for enterprise or franchise brands that need to monitor visibility across many locations at once. Its feature set centers on six core modules: Search Performance, a competitive local and organic rank tracker across major search engines; Geo-Matrix, a map-grid visualization that shows how search rankings shift block by block or across a city, a capability the company markets as more granular than many competitors offer; GBP Radar, which monitors Google Business Profile listings for changes and issues; Reputation Monitoring, which aggregates and tracks reviews across 20 or more review sites in one dashboard; Citation Intel, which discovers where a business is mentioned or cited online; and Listing Audit, which checks NAP (name, address, phone) consistency across directories. Rather than publishing fixed pricing tiers, Places Scout sells custom packages sized to the number of locations and keywords a client needs to track, marketed under the pitch that agencies pay only for the tools they actually use; prospective customers must request a demo or quote to see pricing. User reviews describe the interface as somewhat dated compared to newer competitors, but praise the depth of the Geo-Matrix reporting, the accuracy of the underlying rank data, and a level of personal, responsive customer support that is less common among larger local SEO platforms. Places Scout competes in the local SEO and rank-tracking category alongside tools such as BrightLocal, Whitespark, Moz Local and Local Falcon, which offer overlapping geo-grid rank tracking, citation management and reputation monitoring capabilities for agencies and multi-location brands.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Places Scout is a local SEO software platform focused on giving agencies and multi-location brands visibility into how their businesses rank, are reviewed, and are cited across the web.
In operation since 2011, the platform is built around automated data collection, drawing on more than 400 data points per business to power its rank tracking, reputation and citation tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Its Search Performance and Geo-Matrix tools track local and organic rankings and visualize them on a map grid, showing how visibility changes across a service area down to the block level.

GBP Radar, Reputation Monitoring, Citation Intel and Listing Audit round out the platform, covering Google Business Profile health, multi-site review tracking, citation discovery and NAP consistency checks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Places Scout does not publish fixed pricing tiers; instead, it sells custom packages sized to the number of locations and keywords a customer needs to track.
Prospective customers request a demo or quote directly from the company rather than signing up through a self-serve pricing page.

features.title: Search Performance rank tracking
features.description: Tracks local and organic search rankings against named competitors across major search engines.

features.title: Geo-Matrix visualization
features.description: Displays ranking visibility on a map grid to show how results change block by block or city by city.

features.title: GBP Radar
features.description: Monitors Google Business Profile listings for changes and issues.

features.title: Reputation monitoring
features.description: Aggregates and tracks reviews across 20 or more review sites in one dashboard.

features.title: Citation Intel
features.description: Discovers where a business is mentioned or cited online.

features.title: Listing Audit
features.description: Checks NAP (name, address, phone) consistency across directories and listings.

pros: Deep, granular Geo-Matrix visualization not offered by every local SEO competitor
Covers rank tracking, reviews, citations and listing audits in one platform
Custom, modular pricing lets agencies pay only for the tools they use
Personal, high-touch customer support frequently praised by users
cons: Pricing is not published and requires contacting sales for a quote
Interface has been described by some reviewers as dated
Less brand recognition than larger local SEO platforms
Custom packages can make cost comparison against competitors harder

faqs.question: What does Places Scout do?
faqs.answer: It is local SEO software that tracks search rankings, monitors Google Business Profiles, aggregates reviews, and audits citations and listing consistency for businesses with one or many locations.

faqs.question: Who is Places Scout for?
faqs.answer: Primarily marketing agencies managing multiple client locations and enterprise brands with many physical locations.

faqs.question: How much does Places Scout cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is custom and based on the number of locations and keywords tracked; you must request a quote or demo from the company.

faqs.question: What is Geo-Matrix?
faqs.answer: A feature that visualizes local search rankings on a map grid, showing how visibility changes from block to block or across a city.

faqs.question: Does Places Scout track review sites?
faqs.answer: Yes, it monitors reviews across 20 or more major review platforms alongside Google.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Places Scout?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include BrightLocal, Whitespark, Moz Local and Local Falcon.

faqs.question: Does Places Scout check business listing accuracy?
faqs.answer: Yes, its Listing Audit feature verifies NAP (name, address, phone) consistency across directories.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial?
faqs.answer: Places Scout offers a free demo upon request rather than a self-serve free trial.

use_cases.description: Brands with many physical locations monitor reviews and Google Business Profile health across all sites.

enrichment.short_description: Planable is a collaboration-first social media content planning, approval and publishing platform that lets teams and agencies plan, review and schedule posts across major social networks.
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (acquired by SE Ranking in August 2025)
enrichment.seo_title: Planable Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Planable review: collaborative social media planning and approval platform. Compare features, pricing plans and top scheduling alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Planable is a social media management platform built around team and client collaboration, letting marketing teams and agencies plan, review, approve and publish content across multiple social networks from one workspace. The company was founded in 2016 by Xenia Muntean, Nicolae Gudumac and Vlad Calus in Chisinau, Moldova, and raised a small seed round of roughly $120,000 from investors including Angular Ventures, Fribourg Capital and GapMinder VC. In August 2025, Planable was acquired by SE Ranking, an SEO software platform used by over a million marketers, and now operates as a standalone product within SE Ranking's broader ecosystem. Planable's core differentiator is its collaboration and approval layer: rather than functioning primarily as a scheduler, it offers a visual content calendar and native feed-style previews so teams can see exactly how a post will look on each platform, real-time comments and inline suggestions for editing copy or creative, and customizable approval workflows that can be optional, required or run through multiple sign-off levels before publishing. It supports scheduling and publishing to Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn, X (Twitter), YouTube, Pinterest and Google Business Profile, and organizes work into multi-brand workspaces with labels and campaigns for agencies handling several clients. Add-on modules extend the platform with analytics and reporting, and a unified social inbox for managing comments and direct messages with sentiment-based prioritization and AI-assisted replies. Pricing is subscription-based and billed per workspace: the Basic plan starts at $33/workspace/month for 60 posts and 4 social pages, Pro costs $49/workspace/month for 150 posts and 10 social pages plus API access, and Enterprise is custom-priced with unlimited posts, SSO and dedicated support; Analytics and Social Inbox are separately priced add-ons available on any plan. There is no permanent free tier, only a 50-post free trial. Planable targets marketing agencies managing client accounts, multi-brand and multi-location companies, and in-house marketing teams that need structured content review before anything goes live. Its main competitors in the broader social media management category include Buffer, Hootsuite, Later and Sprout Social, though Planable is most often chosen specifically for its stronger collaboration and client-approval workflow rather than pure scheduling breadth.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Planable is a social media management platform focused on team and client collaboration, letting marketers plan, review, approve and publish content across multiple networks from a shared workspace.

Founded in 2016 in Chisinau, Moldova, by Xenia Muntean, Nicolae Gudumac and Vlad Calus, Planable was acquired by SEO platform SE Ranking in August 2025 and now runs as a standalone product within that company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform's calendar and native feed previews show exactly how a post will appear on each network, while comments, suggestions and customizable approval workflows let teams and clients review content before it goes live.

Planable supports scheduling across Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn, X, YouTube, Pinterest and Google Business Profile, with multi-brand workspaces, labels and campaigns for agencies managing several clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pricing is billed per workspace: Basic starts at $33/month for 60 posts and 4 social pages, Pro is $49/month for 150 posts and 10 social pages, and Enterprise is custom-priced with unlimited posts and SSO.
Analytics and Social Inbox are available as separate paid add-ons on any plan, and there is no permanent free tier, only a 50-post free trial with no credit card required.

features.description: Track performance metrics, audience insights and best-time-to-post recommendations.

features.description: Organize campaigns, labels and content separately across multiple brands or clients.

Unlimited users included on all plans
Native feed preview shows how posts will actually look per platform
Public API and integrations available on higher tiers
cons: No permanent free plan, only a post-limited free trial
Analytics and Social Inbox are priced as separate add-ons rather than bundled

Enterprise-grade features like SSO and dedicated support require custom pricing

faqs.answer: No, Planable offers a free trial of 50 posts with no time limit rather than a permanent free tier.

faqs.question: How much does Planable cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at $33/workspace/month for Basic, with Pro at $49/workspace/month and custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.answer: Planable was acquired by SE Ranking, an SEO software platform, in August 2025.

faqs.answer: Planable emphasizes real-time collaboration and structured approval workflows for teams and clients, rather than focusing primarily on scheduling.

faqs.answer: Planable was founded in 2016 by Xenia Muntean, Nicolae Gudumac and Vlad Calus in Chisinau, Moldova.

enrichment.short_description: Plane (plane.com) is a Y Combinator-backed global payroll, HR and compliance platform for hiring, paying and managing US employees, international contractors and Employer-of-Record workers from one API-first, agent-ready system. It is a distinct company from the open-source project management tool also called Plane, hosted at plane.so.
enrichment.starting_price: $0/month for HRIS; $19-$499 per person/month depending on worker type
enrichment.seo_title: Plane (plane.com) Review 2026: Payroll and HR Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Plane review: Y Combinator-backed global payroll, HR and EOR platform for contractors and employees. See pricing, features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Plane, at plane.com, is a global payroll, HR and compliance platform for hiring, paying and managing US employees, international contractors and Employer-of-Record (EOR) workers from a single system. The company was founded in 2017 by Staszek Kolarzowski and Matt Pelc and went through Y Combinator's Winter 2017 (W17) batch; it is headquartered in San Francisco, California. Plane's core product unifies an HRIS, US payroll, global contractor payments (in 240+ countries and 70+ local currencies) and EOR hiring (in 100+ countries) into one platform, with automated compliance features such as multi-state payroll tax registration, W-8/W-9 collection, 1099 filing and locally compliant contract generation. Its more recent positioning leans into agentic automation: Plane exposes an open API, CLI and MCP (Model Context Protocol) support so that AI agents, including tools like Claude, can perform payroll and HR actions directly, and it offers a Slack-based Plane Agent for real-time payroll and HR queries. Pricing is usage-based and transparent: the HRIS layer is free for all worker types, US W-2 employees cost $19/month, international contractors cost $39/month, and EOR hires cost $499/month, with no setup fees or long-term contracts. Plane targets fast-growing startups and remote-first companies that need to hire globally without setting up local legal entities, as well as engineering and product teams that want programmatic, agent-callable access to payroll and HR data. Important disambiguation: plane.com is operated by a different company from Plane Project Management, the popular open-source Jira and Linear alternative available at plane.so, founded in 2022 in Hyderabad and San Francisco and backed by OSS Capital. The two products share only a name and coincidentally similar branding; they have separate founders, separate funding histories and serve entirely different markets, payroll and HR versus project management. Plane's payroll-category competitors include Deel, Remote, Rippling and Papaya Global, all of which offer overlapping EOR, contractor payment and global payroll capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Plane, at plane.com, is a global payroll and HR platform that lets companies hire, pay and manage US employees, international contractors and Employer-of-Record workers from one system. It was founded in 2017 by Staszek Kolarzowski and Matt Pelc, went through Y Combinator's W17 batch, and is based in San Francisco.

Note for buyers: this is a different company from the well-known open-source project management tool also named Plane, which is hosted at plane.so, founded separately in 2022 and unrelated to this payroll business beyond sharing a name.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Plane combines an HRIS, US W-2 payroll, global contractor payments in 240+ countries, and Employer-of-Record hiring in 100+ countries into one platform, with automated compliance such as tax registration, 1099 filing and locally compliant contracts.
It leans into agentic automation with an open API, CLI and MCP support that let AI agents perform payroll and HR actions directly, plus a Slack-based Plane Agent assistant for real-time queries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Plane prices per person per month based on worker type: the HRIS is free, US employees cost $19/month, international contractors cost $39/month, and EOR hires cost $499/month.
There are no setup fees, minimum charges or long-term contracts, and customers can cancel at any time.

pros: Transparent, pay-per-person pricing with no setup fees or minimums
Combines HRIS, US payroll, global contractors and EOR in one platform
Early mover in exposing payroll and HR actions to AI agents via MCP and CLI
Backed by Y Combinator with an established operating history since 2017
cons: EOR pricing at $499/person/month is a significant recurring cost for smaller teams
Company details such as funding and team size are not fully disclosed publicly
The newer agentic positioning means some AI-driven features are still maturing
Shares its name with an unrelated, well-known open-source project management tool, which can cause buyer confusion

faqs.question: Is Plane (plane.com) the same as the Plane project management tool?
faqs.answer: No, they are unrelated companies that happen to share the name Plane. Plane.com is a payroll and HR platform founded in 2017 (YC W17); the open-source project management tool called Plane is hosted at plane.so and was founded separately in 2022.

faqs.answer: Yes, Plane offers an open API, CLI and MCP support so AI agents can perform payroll and HR tasks, plus a Slack-based Plane Agent assistant.

faqs.answer: Plane supports contractor payments in 240+ countries and EOR hiring in 100+ countries.

faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Deel, Remote, Rippling and Papaya Global.

enrichment.short_description: Plane (at plane.so) is an open-source, AI-native project management platform and Jira/Linear alternative for tracking work items, sprints, docs and workflows, available as a managed cloud service or self-hosted. It is a separate company from the payroll and HR platform also called Plane, at plane.com.
enrichment.starting_price: Free for up to 12 seats; paid plans from $6/seat/month
enrichment.headquarters: Hyderabad, India and Delaware/San Francisco, United States (dual headquarters)
enrichment.seo_title: Plane Review 2026: Open-Source PM Features and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Plane (plane.so) review: open-source Jira and Linear alternative with work items, cycles, docs and AI. Compare pricing, features and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Plane, at plane.so and maintained under the GitHub organization makeplane, is an open-source, AI-native project management platform positioned as an alternative to Jira, Linear, Asana, Monday.com and ClickUp. It was founded in late 2022 by brothers Vamsi Kurama (CEO) and Vihar Kurama (COO), operating with a dual headquarters structure spanning Hyderabad, India and a Delaware/San Francisco US entity. The project launched publicly via its makeplane/plane GitHub repository, quickly gained developer traction as a lightweight, self-hostable alternative to Jira's complexity, and has grown to tens of thousands of GitHub stars, making it one of the most starred open-source project management tools available. In April 2024, Plane raised a $4 million seed round led by OSS Capital, the dedicated open-source-focused fund founded by Joseph Jacks; this remains its primary disclosed institutional funding. The product is released under the AGPL-3.0 license and is built with TypeScript/React on the frontend and Python/Django on the backend, and can be deployed as a fully managed Plane Cloud service or self-hosted via Docker or Kubernetes, including air-gapped enterprise deployments. Core capabilities include work items (issues) with rich-text editing, Cycles for sprint tracking with burndown charts, Modules for breaking large projects into components, customizable views and filters, a built-in Wiki for documentation linked to work items, and Plane AI for AI-assisted workflows and automation. Pricing follows a freemium, per-seat model: a Free tier supports up to 12 users with core project management features and 500 AI credits per seat; Pro costs $6/seat/month with unlimited seats and expanded AI credits, wiki, time tracking and integrations; Business costs $13/seat/month adding templates, intake forms, customer profiles and advanced dashboards; and Enterprise Grid is custom-priced for private and managed deployments, SSO/LDAP, audit logs and migration services. Plane targets engineering, product and startup teams that want an open-source, self-hostable Jira/Linear alternative with modern UX. Its main competitors are Jira, Linear, ClickUp, Asana and Monday.com. Note: despite sharing the name Plane, this project management product is an entirely separate company from the payroll and HR platform found at plane.com, which was founded independently in 2017 in San Francisco and has no ownership relationship with plane.so.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Plane, at plane.so, is an open-source, AI-native project management platform positioned as an alternative to Jira, Linear, Asana and ClickUp. It was founded in late 2022 by brothers Vamsi Kurama and Vihar Kurama, operating out of Hyderabad, India and a US entity in Delaware/San Francisco.
Note for buyers: this is a different company from Plane at plane.com, a global payroll and HR platform founded independently in 2017; the two products share only a name.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Plane organizes work into issues, Cycles for sprint tracking with burndown charts, and Modules for breaking large projects into components, alongside customizable board, list, spreadsheet and timeline views.

It includes a built-in Wiki linked to work items and Plane AI for AI-assisted workflows, and can be deployed as a managed Plane Cloud service or self-hosted via Docker or Kubernetes, including air-gapped enterprise setups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Plane follows a freemium, per-seat model: the Free tier supports up to 12 users, Pro is $6/seat/month, and Business is $13/seat/month, each with an increasing allocation of AI credits per seat.
Enterprise Grid is custom-priced for organizations that need private or managed deployments, SSO/LDAP, audit logs and migration support.

features.title: Work items and issue tracking

features.title: Cycles
features.description: Sprint-style cycles with burndown charts and velocity tracking.

features.title: Modules
features.description: Break large projects into manageable components or feature areas.

features.title: Multiple views
features.description: Board, list, spreadsheet, Gantt/timeline and calendar views with custom filters.

features.title: Built-in Wiki
features.description: Documentation and pages linked directly to project work items.

features.title: Plane AI
features.description: AI-assisted work item creation, summaries and workflow automation.

features.title: Self-hosted or cloud deployment
features.description: Deploy via Docker/Kubernetes for full data control, or use the managed Plane Cloud service.

pros: Open source (AGPL-3.0) with a free self-hosted option and full source access
Generous free cloud tier for up to 12 seats
Modern, fast interface compared to legacy tools like Jira
Self-hosting and air-gapped deployment support enterprise and compliance needs
Backed by OSS Capital, a fund focused on sustainable open-source businesses
cons: Younger product, founded in 2022, with a smaller feature set than mature tools like Jira in some enterprise areas
AI credits are metered per seat, which can add complexity to cost planning
Self-hosting requires infrastructure management
Smaller third-party integration and app marketplace than Atlassian's Jira ecosystem

faqs.question: Is Plane (plane.so) free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plane offers a free cloud tier for up to 12 seats, plus a fully free self-hosted option since the software is open source.

faqs.question: Who owns Plane?
faqs.answer: Plane is developed by a company founded by Vamsi Kurama and Vihar Kurama in 2022, backed by OSS Capital.

faqs.question: Is Plane the same company as the payroll platform Plane at plane.com?
faqs.answer: No, they are unrelated companies that happen to share the name Plane; plane.so is the project management tool, and plane.com is a separate payroll and HR company founded in 2017.

faqs.question: Can Plane be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plane is released under the AGPL-3.0 license and can be self-hosted via Docker or Kubernetes, including air-gapped deployments.

faqs.question: What are Plane's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Jira, Linear, ClickUp, Asana and Monday.com.

faqs.question: Does Plane have AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plane AI provides AI-assisted work item creation and workflow automation, with AI credits included per seat.

faqs.question: How much does Plane cost?
faqs.answer: Free for up to 12 seats; Pro is $6/seat/month, Business is $13/seat/month, and Enterprise Grid is custom-priced.

faqs.question: Has Plane raised funding?
faqs.answer: Yes, Plane raised a $4 million seed round led by OSS Capital in April 2024.

enrichment.short_description: PlanetScale is a cloud database platform offering managed, horizontally scalable MySQL (via Vitess) and PostgreSQL databases with Git-style branching, built by the original creators of Vitess.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: PlanetScale review: managed Vitess-based MySQL and Postgres database platform with branching. Compare pricing, features and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PlanetScale is a cloud database platform that provides fully managed, horizontally scalable MySQL databases built on Vitess, alongside a newer managed PostgreSQL product. It was founded in 2018 by Jiten Vaidya and Sugu Sougoumarane, the original co-creators and maintainers of the open-source Vitess project, which they built at YouTube starting around 2010 to scale MySQL to petabyte scale across tens of thousands of nodes. The company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and has raised over $100 million across multiple rounds, including a $3 million seed in 2018 led by SignalFire, a $22 million Series A led by Andreessen Horowitz, a $30 million Series B led by Insight Partners, and a $50 million Series C led by Kleiner Perkins. PlanetScale's core technical differentiator is Vitess-powered horizontal sharding combined with Git-style, non-blocking schema branching and online schema changes, which let developers create isolated database branches, test schema changes safely, and merge them without downtime; the platform also offers Query Insights, connection pooling, and SOC 2 Type 2, HIPAA and PCI DSS Level 1 compliance. In March 2024, PlanetScale discontinued its free Hobby tier and reduced staff as part of a strategic shift away from the self-serve hobbyist and indie-developer segment toward enterprise customers, and it has since expanded into managed PostgreSQL, including a bare-metal PlanetScale Metal NVMe offering, alongside its original Vitess/MySQL product. Pricing today starts around $5/month for a single-node Postgres development database and $39/month for entry-level Vitess clusters, scaling to tens of thousands of dollars per month for large, high-availability, multi-region deployments. The Vitess technology underlying PlanetScale is used directly by large-scale platforms such as Slack, GitHub, Block and Bloomberg. PlanetScale targets engineering teams at startups and enterprises that need MySQL- or Postgres-compatible databases at scale with modern developer workflows like branching and preview environments. Its main competitors include AWS Aurora, Google Cloud SQL/AlloyDB, Neon, CockroachDB and Supabase.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PlanetScale is a cloud database platform providing fully managed, horizontally scalable MySQL databases built on Vitess, plus a newer managed PostgreSQL product. It was founded in 2018 by Jiten Vaidya and Sugu Sougoumarane, the original creators of Vitess, and is headquartered in San Francisco.

The company has raised over $100 million from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Insight Partners and Kleiner Perkins, and the underlying Vitess technology is used directly by large-scale platforms such as Slack, GitHub, Block and Bloomberg.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: PlanetScale's core differentiator is Vitess-powered horizontal sharding combined with Git-style, non-blocking schema branching, letting developers create isolated database branches and test schema changes safely before merging without downtime.

The platform also offers Query Insights for performance monitoring, connection pooling, high-availability configurations with up to 99.999% SLA, and enterprise-grade compliance including SOC 2 Type 2, HIPAA and PCI DSS Level 1.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: In March 2024, PlanetScale discontinued its free Hobby tier as it shifted focus toward enterprise customers; pricing now starts around $5/month for a single-node Postgres development database and $39/month for entry-level Vitess clusters.
Costs scale with compute, storage and high-availability requirements, reaching into the tens of thousands of dollars per month for large, multi-region, bare-metal deployments.

features.title: Vitess-powered horizontal sharding
features.description: Scale MySQL-compatible databases across many nodes using the same technology that powers YouTube's database layer.

features.title: Managed PostgreSQL
features.description: Fully managed Postgres clusters with high-availability and bare-metal NVMe options.

features.title: Database branching
features.description: Create Git-style, isolated database branches for schema changes and testing before merging to production.

features.title: Online, non-blocking schema changes
features.description: Apply schema changes without downtime or table locks.

features.title: Query Insights
features.description: Built-in monitoring for slow queries and query performance.

features.title: High availability
features.description: Up to 99.999% SLA on multi-region deployments with automatic failover.

features.title: Enterprise-grade security
features.description: SOC 2 Type 2, HIPAA and PCI DSS Level 1 compliance.

pros: Built by the original creators of Vitess, with deep expertise in scaling relational databases
Git-style branching and non-blocking schema changes reduce migration risk
Proven at massive scale, with Vitess powering Slack, GitHub, Block and Bloomberg
Strong compliance posture, including SOC 2, HIPAA and PCI DSS, suits regulated industries
Now offers both Vitess/MySQL and native Postgres products
cons: No free tier since the Hobby plan was discontinued in April 2024, raising the barrier for side projects and small apps
Pricing can scale quickly for high-availability, multi-region deployments
Shift toward enterprise focus may be less attractive to solo developers and small teams
Vitess sharding model adds architectural complexity compared to a plain managed database

faqs.question: Does PlanetScale have a free tier?
faqs.answer: No, PlanetScale discontinued its free Hobby tier in April 2024; all current plans are paid, starting around $5/month for a small Postgres database.

faqs.question: Who founded PlanetScale?
faqs.answer: PlanetScale was founded in 2018 by Jiten Vaidya and Sugu Sougoumarane, the original creators of Vitess.

faqs.question: What is Vitess?
faqs.answer: Vitess is an open-source database clustering system for horizontal scaling of MySQL, originally built at YouTube; PlanetScale is built and maintained by its creators.

faqs.question: Does PlanetScale support PostgreSQL?
faqs.answer: Yes, alongside its original Vitess/MySQL product, PlanetScale offers managed PostgreSQL, including a high-performance PlanetScale Metal NVMe option.

faqs.question: What is database branching?
faqs.answer: A workflow that lets developers create isolated copies of a database schema, like Git branches, to test changes safely before deploying to production.

faqs.question: Is PlanetScale suitable for regulated industries?
faqs.answer: Yes, it holds SOC 2 Type 2, HIPAA and PCI DSS Level 1 compliance certifications.

faqs.question: How much does PlanetScale cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing starts around $5/month for a small non-HA Postgres instance and $39/month for entry-level Vitess clusters, scaling up based on compute, storage and high-availability needs.

faqs.question: What are PlanetScale's main competitors?
faqs.answer: AWS Aurora, Google Cloud SQL/AlloyDB, Neon, CockroachDB and Supabase.

use_cases.title: Scaling MySQL at high traffic volume
use_cases.description: Engineering teams migrate to PlanetScale's Vitess-based sharding to scale MySQL-compatible workloads beyond a single database instance.

use_cases.title: Safe schema migrations via branching
use_cases.description: Development teams use database branches to test and review schema changes before merging into production, similar to a Git workflow.

use_cases.title: Regulated, compliance-sensitive workloads
use_cases.description: Companies in healthcare, finance and other regulated industries run PlanetScale for its SOC 2, HIPAA and PCI DSS compliance.

features: Scales up to 256 GiB and beyond
Cluster plus storage plus backups plus egress
Configurable storage up to approximately 15 TiB
